advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'read-only / no execution' framing with the fact that member resolution imports and runs arbitrary module code, or restrict member binding to a non-executing mechanism.
evidence: environment.py module docstring claims resolution consults the environment '(without executing it)' for find_spec, but member binding imports the package and runs its `__init__`

pai_tools.py:151-152: "Read-only (import-locating; a member bind imports the module) and fail-open (never raises into the agent loop)." — 'Read-only' understates that a member bind executes arbitrary import-time code
resolve.py:479-482: the member branch calls `_refute_via_environment(ref)` inside the oracle, so import-time execution happens server-side, not only in the agent sandbox
advisory.finding: Member/attribute resolution imports and EXECUTES arbitrary module top-level code via `importlib.import_module`, contradicting the 'no execution / read-only' framing in the tool and module docstrings. This runs server-side inside the grounding oracle (refute_via_environment) and via the agent's resolve_symbol tool.
scenarios: A reviewed diff references `module.attr` where `module` has import-time side effects (network, file writes, heavy initialization) present in the resolution environment

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the commit message to reflect that the always-on third-party proof uses PyYAML (yaml), not pydantic.
evidence: commit body: 'Tests (deterministic, no API key) use a real installed dep (pydantic.BaseModel under site-packages)'
test module docstring: 'Third-party symbol under test: ``yaml`` / ``yaml.safe_load`` (PyYAML) — a CORE runtime dependency'
advisory.finding: The commit message states the tests use `pydantic.BaseModel under site-packages` as the deterministic proof, but the always-on test dependency is actually `yaml`/PyYAML; pydantic cases are importorskip-guarded [agents]-only. The commit description is stale relative to the test code.
scenarios: A reader relying on the commit message to understand the test's always-on proof is misled about which dependency guarantees coverage

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a resolve_symbol test that resolves a repo-local module (origin under repo_path) and asserts the EXISTS output contains '[repo-local'.
evidence: pai_tools.py:180-181: `inside = _within_root(os.path.realpath(origin), root) if os.path.isabs(origin) else False` / `scope = "repo-local" if inside else "third-party/stdlib (site-packages)"`
tool docstring advertises the branch: pai_tools.py:160-161 `Returns EXISTS (with origin + whether it is repo-local or third-party)`
tests only assert the third-party path: test_resolve_symbol_tool.py:32 `assert "third-party/stdlib" in out` and :38 `assert "yaml.safe_load" in out` — no case resolves an in-repo module
ABSENCE: no test passes a repo_path + a name resolving to a module under that root to assert an `EXISTS: ... [repo-local, ...]` output
advisory.finding: The resolve_symbol tool's 'repo-local' scope branch (the EXISTS output labeling an in-repo origin) is never exercised: all tool tests resolve `yaml` (third-party) or unresolvable names, so the _within_root / os.path.isabs scope-classification path at pai_tools.py:180-181 is untested. A regression that mislabels a repo-local symbol as third-party/stdlib (or vice versa) would not be caught.
scenarios: A future edit inverts the _within_root result or drops the os.path.isabs guard, causing a repo-local symbol to be reported as 'third-party/stdlib' — tests stay green.
advisory.suggested_fix: Add a test that calls grounding_tools(<repo root>) and resolves an in-repo module (e.g. a rebar submodule whose file lives under the given root), asserting the returned string starts with 'EXISTS' and contains 'repo-local'.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Make `resolve_symbol` resolution non-executing (or dependency-allowlisted) so attacker-influenced module names named by the reviewing agent cannot trigger arbitrary module imports.
evidence: pai_tools.py:168 `loc = _resolve.resolve_in_environment(name, container=module or None, language="python")` — agent-supplied `name`/`module` flow into the environment resolver (which imports modules).
runner.py:239-245 wires `grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path)` into the live multi-turn reviewer tool surface alongside the sandboxed FS tools.

code-review-base.md:20-23 instructs: 'use `resolve_symbol` to confirm it in the installed environment before treating it as undefined/hallucinated' — steering the agent to invoke it on diff-derived symbol names.
plan_review_G1G2.md and plan_review_E4.md add the same directive to call `resolve_symbol` on symbols not found via Grep.
pai_tools.py:43-96 by contrast the FS tools are realpath-checked, deny-listed, and repo-confined — `resolve_symbol` intentionally has NO such confinement (it imports from site-packages / sys.path).
advisory.finding: The `resolve_symbol` agent tool deliberately reaches outside the repo sandbox and imports modules the LLM names, while the reviewer prompts instruct the agent to call it on any symbol it cannot find in the untrusted diff — giving attacker-controlled diff content a path to trigger module imports (code execution) in the live reviewer.
scenarios: A malicious PR includes an import/reference to a module the attacker also placed in the reviewed snapshot (repo-local) or an installed dependency; the reviewer agent, following prompt guidance, calls resolve_symbol on it and the module is imported/executed.
